skill.name: testing-with-sandbox
skill.description: Guides development and QA teams through Mailtrap Email Sandbox setup for safe outbound email capture, inspection, and automated testing without delivering messages to real recipients.
skill.summary: Test outbound email safely with Mailtrap Email Sandbox.
skill.author: mailtrap

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby or shell execution are false positives caused by markdown inline code, token names, and endpoint examples. The skill intentionally documents Mailtrap API, SMTP, and email sandbox workflows, so network and email-sending risk is real but scoped to test inbox usage. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malware intent, or executable code was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Mailtrap Network and Email Testing Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to send and inspect test email through Mailtrap Sandbox API and SMTP endpoints. This is the core purpose of the skill and appears legitimate, but it can transmit test message content and requires users to handle sandbox tokens and credentials correctly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The markdown explicitly documents Mailtrap Sandbox API and SMTP endpoints plus token usage. The behavior is expected for an email sandbox skill, but network transmission and credential handling are confirmed.

low_findings.title: False Positive Command Execution Matches
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the file is markdown documentation. The matched text is inline code, related skill names, endpoint examples, token placeholders, and URLs; no executable Ruby, shell script, or command invocation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md contains documentation only and the flagged backticks are markdown formatting. No code block or file in the analysis results executes those strings.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code or host inspection. The cited lines describe the skill metadata, documentation links, account_id resolution through Mailtrap APIs, and sandbox endpoint selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I found no hashing, encryption, local system probing, or host reconnaissance logic in the cited markdown. The only runtime lookup described is a documented Mailtrap account API request.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Mailtrap Documentation and Service URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Mailtrap documentation, official SDK repositories, and Mailtrap sandbox service endpoints. They are expected for setup guidance, but users should verify current endpoint details before production-adjacent use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and align with Mailtrap documentation and repositories. This is a legitimate documentation pattern, though endpoint staleness is possible.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Outbound Email and API Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes specific Mailtrap sandbox API, SMTP, and inbox URLs that users may call when following the instructions. This is intentional and scoped to sandbox testing, but it remains a network-enabled workflow that can handle test email content and credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and SMTP host examples are explicit. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because the skill is documentation for an email testing service.

content.user_title: Test Email Safely with Mailtrap Sandbox
content.value_statement: Outbound email tests can accidentally reach real recipients or expose broken templates late. This skill helps teams route mail into Mailtrap Sandbox, inspect messages, and automate checks before live sending.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Mailtrap Email Sandbox instead of live sending.
Lists sandbox SMTP host, supported ports, and credential source.
Documents common Sandbox API paths for inboxes and messages.
Clarifies token scope, inbox id, and account_id requirements.
Points users to official SDK repositories for current sandbox options.
Identifies common mistakes when mixing sandbox and transactional endpoints.
limitations: Does not provide full framework-specific setup guides.
Does not replace current Mailtrap API documentation or SDK README files.
Requires valid Mailtrap sandbox credentials and scoped API tokens.
Does not deliver messages to real recipients from the sandbox.

use_cases.title: Protect Staging Recipients
use_cases.description: Route application email from development or staging into a sandbox inbox before enabling live delivery.

use_cases.title: Validate Message Quality
use_cases.description: Inspect headers, HTML, attachments, and spam signals for captured test messages.

use_cases.title: Automate Email Assertions
use_cases.description: Use Sandbox API paths to fetch captured messages and verify expected email behavior in CI.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Sandbox Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me decide whether to use Mailtrap Sandbox SMTP or the Sandbox API for my development environment.

prompt_templates.title: Configure SMTP Testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the Mailtrap Sandbox SMTP settings I need and explain where to find the sandbox credentials.

prompt_templates.title: Plan API Message Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a test flow that sends email to Mailtrap Sandbox, lists captured messages, and checks the latest message content.

prompt_templates.title: Review CI Email Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my CI email testing plan for Mailtrap Sandbox and identify token, inbox id, and endpoint mistakes.

output_examples.input: I need to test password reset emails without sending them to customers.
output_examples.output: Use Mailtrap Email Sandbox in development or staging. Configure SMTP or the Sandbox API, send the reset email, then inspect the captured message in the test inbox.

output_examples.input: My tests need to confirm that an invoice email was generated.
output_examples.output: Send the invoice email to the sandbox, list messages for the inbox, fetch the latest message, and assert the subject, recipient, and body content.

output_examples.input: I am unsure which token to use for sandbox testing.
output_examples.output: Use a Mailtrap token with sandbox or testing scope. Do not reuse a live sending token for sandbox workflows.

best_practices: Keep sandbox tokens separate from live sending tokens.
Resolve the correct account_id and inbox id before writing automation.
Check current SDK README files before generating language-specific code.
anti_patterns: Using sandbox endpoints in production email delivery paths.
Expecting sandbox messages to reach real recipients.
Mixing transactional sending endpoints with sandbox testing endpoints.

faq.question: Does Mailtrap Email Sandbox send email to real users?
faq.answer: No. Sandbox messages are captured in test inboxes and are not delivered to real recipients.

faq.question: When should I use SMTP instead of the Sandbox API?
faq.answer: Use SMTP when your application already sends mail through SMTP settings. Use the API for new integrations and automation.

faq.question: What credentials do I need?
faq.answer: You need sandbox SMTP credentials or a Mailtrap token with sandbox or testing scope.

faq.question: What is the inbox id used for?
faq.answer: The inbox id identifies the sandbox test inbox that should receive or store the message.

faq.question: Can this skill generate complete framework guides?
faq.answer: No. It points users to Mailtrap documentation and SDK README files for detailed framework steps.

faq.question: Can I use the same endpoint for sandbox and live email?
faq.answer: No. Sandbox and live transactional sending use different endpoints and different operational expectations.
